perturbation.original_text: A. Supplier shall use Confidential Information only to assist Supplier in making propos als to provide Supplies to Company and in providing Supplies to Company unde r Company’s purchase orders or other written agreements. Supplier shall not use or disclose Confidential Information except as required by the performance of its obligations to Company.
perturbation.changed_text: A. Supplier shall use Confidential Information only to assist Supplier in making proposals to provide Supplies to Company and in providing Supplies to Company. Supplier may use or disclose Confidential Information without limitations.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'under Company’s purchase orders or other written agreements' and 'Supplier shall not use or disclose Confidential Information except as required by the performance of its obligations to Company', the changed text now permits the Supplier to use the Confidential Information without any constraints related to existing agreements or obligations. This contradicts Section 2.E, which prohibits the supplier from providing supplies to anyone other than the company if the supplies include confidential information. The omission removes critical limitations on the use of confidential information, creating uncertainty regarding enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: E. During and after the period Supplier is providing Supplier services to Company, Supplier shall not produc e, provide, or sell to anyone , other than Company, any Supplies which include or are produc ed with Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section A removes limitations on the Supplier's use of Confidential Information, specifically the requirement that it be used only under Company's purchase orders or other written agreements and that Supplier shall not use or disclose Confidential Information except as required by the performance of its obligations to Company. This directly contradicts section E, which prohibits the supplier from providing supplies including confidential information to anyone other than the company. The changed text now allows unrestricted use, conflicting with the exclusivity clause in section E.

perturbation.original_text: If Company accepts Supplier as an approved supplier for Supplies, any supply of such Supplies shall be unde r Company’s standard General Terms and Conditions of Purchase without the need for further adoption of such terms and conditions.
perturbation.changed_text: If Company accepts Supplier as an approved supplier for Supplies, any supply of such Supplies shall be subject to mutually agreed upon terms.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that any supply of goods is under 'Company’s standard General Terms and Conditions of Purchase', providing clarity and a pre-established framework. By changing it to 'mutually agreed upon terms', the perturbation introduces ambiguity, as there is no guarantee what those terms will be. This contradicts Section 4.C, which states that the agreement 'constitutes the entire agreement between the parties', creating uncertainty about which set of terms actually governs the supply of goods. The deletion implies that the General Terms and Conditions of Purchase doesn't apply.
perturbation.contradicted_text: C. This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties respecting the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior agreements between the parties relating to the subject matter hereof, whether written or oral. It is effective on execution by Supplier. No waiver or modification of the terms of this Agreement shall be valid unless in writing and duly executed by Supplier and Company. This Agreement shall be cumulative with all other rights of Company unde r any existing or future agreements between Company and Supplier unless specifically waived by Company.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that any supply of goods will be under the Company's standard General Terms and Conditions. The changed text replaces this with 'mutually agreed upon terms', introducing ambiguity and contradicting section C, which states that the agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties. This creates uncertainty about which set of terms governs the supply of goods and directly conflicts with the 'entire agreement' clause, fitting the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: F. In addition, Supplier shall not and warrants that anyone obtain the Confidential Information disclosed hereunde r by or through it, shall not: (i) Contest, anywhere in the world at any time in the future, the validity of Company’s proprietary rights in, or any Company claim to the ownership or inventorship of, any Confidential Information; or (ii) Attempt to or actually apply for, obtain or claim ownership of, inventorship in or right to use of any proprietary rights in any Confidential Information (including, without limitation, any patents, copyrights, trademarks, service marks, trade dress, or trade secret rights).
perturbation.changed_text: F. In addition, Supplier warrants that it will respect Company's intellectual property rights.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause F contains a specific and detailed commitment from the supplier not to challenge the company's proprietary rights or attempt to claim ownership of confidential information. The modified clause, 'Supplier warrants that it will respect Company's intellectual property rights', is much more general. The original provides a very strong commitment and consequences associated to it, while the modified text creates uncertanity on the actions that the supplier can do, creating intext contradictions. Because the term is now ambiguous, this contradicts Section 4.D, which refers to enforcement actions and classes of protectible information. It creates an ambiguity about the extent of protection.
perturbation.contradicted_text: D. This Agreement shall be construed unde r the laws of the State of Michigan, notwithstanding principles of Michigan or any other state, provided Company may in any enforcement action elect to have the law of the forum jurisdiction to apply. The parties irrevocably consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the jurisdiction in which Company has an office to resolve all issues related to or arising from this Agreement and any business relationship arising from or related to this Agreement. If the law applicable to any enforcement proceeding limits the class of information and materials protectible by contract to trade secrets or other class of information, the term “Confidential Information” shall be limited to those classes of information and materials. If the law applicable to any enforcement proceeding requires a temporal limitation, the period after termination for which Supplier is restricted in use or disclosure of Confidential Information which is not a trade secret shall be limited to any specific maximum permitted by law or five years from the date of termination. If the law applicable to any enforcement proceeding requires a geographic limitation, the area in which Supplier is restricted in its use or disclosure of Confidential Information shall be limited to only those states and countries in which Company or a supplier or customer of Company shall have an office or other facility during the restricted period. If the law applicable to any enforcement proceeding requires a limitation on the scope of any restriction, such restriction in the Agreement is limited to those activities which Supplier has performed for Company and to those customers of Company with which Supplier has had some contact during the engagement with Company.
perturbation.justification: The original clause F provides specific commitments from the supplier not to challenge the company's proprietary rights, with associated consequences. The changed clause F, 'Supplier warrants that it will respect Company's intellectual property rights', is much more general and lacks the detailed commitment of the original. This creates ambiguity about the extent of protection. Because of this ambiguity, it contradicts section D, which refers to enforcement actions and classes of protectible information. The changed text causes an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: G. All improvements to Company Produc ts, their compone nts, or their unde rlying technology developed during the performance of any purchase order issued by Company to Supplier shall belong to Company.
perturbation.changed_text: G. Ownership of improvements to Company Products will be negotiated on a case-by-case basis.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies that all improvements to Company products developed during the performance of any purchase order issued by Company to Supplier automatically belong to Company. The modification, stating that ownership will be negotiated on a case-by-case basis, introduces uncertainty and contradicts the original intention. The original text is very strong in what happens, while the changed text provides a uncertainty on what will happen and how. This omission creates a contradiction with Section 1.A, which defines 'Confidential Information' to include proprietary information 'owned by or licensed to Company,' as it is unclear if the improvements are still considered Confidential Information if ownership is now negotiable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: A. “Confidential Information” means all trade secrets, proprietary information, know-how, and confidential information of Company including but not limited to: (i) any and all technical, business or financial information or property, owned by or licensed to Company, or otherwise relating to Company and/or any of its subsidiaries, affiliates and related entities which is heretofore or hereinafter disclosed to Supplier, including but not limited to information regarding Company’s goods or services, processes, personne l, finances, business plans, studies, analyses, projections, research, market data, operations, apparatus, computer software, know-how, trade secrets, inventions, equipment, tools, molds, dies, fixtures, parts, prototypes, samples, drawings, test results, material and manufacturing specifications, suppliers, customers, employees, processes, licensing and any other ideas or information relating to Company’s business or Company Produc ts, the Supplies or any business or activity in which Company is engaged, regardless of the form of disclosure, whether or not disclosed in a writing marked “Confidential” or in some similar manner or identified as confidential; (ii) improvements derived by Supplier from the information identified in Subsection l(A)(i) or from access to Company’s facilities; and (iii) any and all software, reports, memoranda, documents, developments, or other results produc ed by Supplier in the performance of providing Supplies that are directly related to Company’s business and not primarily to general technology used by Supplier in the conduc t of its core business.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that all improvements to Company products belong to Company. The changed text introduces the possibility that the ownership of improvements will be negotiated on a case-by-case basis, which contradicts the original clear assignment of ownership. This uncertainty directly conflicts with Section A's definition of 'Confidential Information,' which includes proprietary information 'owned by or licensed to Company.' If ownership is now negotiable, it is unclear whether improvements made by the Supplier are still considered Confidential Information owned by the Company, therefore creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: B. The unenforceability of any provision of this Agreement shall not affect the other provisions and the unenforceable clause shall be deemed deleted and shall be deleted by the court.
perturbation.changed_text: B. If any provision of this Agreement is found to be unenforceable, the parties will attempt to renegotiate the provision in good faith.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that an unenforceable provision will be deleted and the rest of the agreement remains valid. The revised clause suggests renegotiation. This directly contradicts the original intent of maintaining the agreement's validity, creating uncertainty and potentially invalidating other clauses during renegotiation. This creates uncertainty on the current clauses, which can lead to intext contradictions. This creates contradiction with Section 4.C that states 'This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties'. The re-negotiation creates the uncertainty of the full agreement being changed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: C. This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties respecting the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior agreements between the parties relating to the subject matter hereof, whether written or oral. It is effective on execution by Supplier. No waiver or modification of the terms of this Agreement shall be valid unless in writing and duly executed by Supplier and Company. This Agreement shall be cumulative with all other rights of Company unde r any existing or future agreements between Company and Supplier unless specifically waived by Company.
perturbation.justification: The original clause stated that an unenforceable provision would be deleted. The changed clause mandates renegotiation. Section C stipulates that the agreement constitutes the entire agreement and that modifications must be in writing. The renegotiation of a clause introduces uncertainty about the entire agreement, directly contradicting the 'entire agreement' clause and the requirement for written modifications, indicating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: E. The interests of Company in the Confidential Information cannot be adequately protected by a remedy at law. This Agreement may be enforced by preliminary injunction as well as other available equitable and legal remedies.
perturbation.changed_text: E. The Company can pursue legal remedies for breaches of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies that the Company's interests in Confidential Information cannot be adequately protected by a remedy at law and allows for enforcement by preliminary injunction. The modified clause only states the Company can pursue 'legal remedies,' removing the specific mention of preliminary injunction and the inadequacy of legal remedies alone. The omission of preliminary injunction directly contradicts the original intent of providing stronger protection for confidential information than standard legal remedies. This creates a contradiction to the statement that all Confidential Information is protectible, generating an intext contradiction that can affect the enforceability of the terms.
perturbation.contradicted_text: C. All Confidential Information is acknow ledged by Supplier to be the property or an otherwise protectible interest of Company or its licensors whether or not it constitutes a trade secret.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that legal remedies are inadequate to protect the Company's interests in Confidential Information, allowing for preliminary injunctions. The changed text removes the mention of preliminary injunctions and only states the Company can pursue 'legal remedies'. This contradicts the contradicted text ('All Confidential Information is acknow ledged by Supplier to be the property or an otherwise protectible interest of Company') by implying that standard legal remedies are sufficient, weakening the protection of Confidential Information. This omission creates a direct conflict, making the enforceability of protecting the confidential information uncertain.
